behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: local module wiring (CSV row parser -> schema validator -> DLQ writer with row metadata propagation). The failure is at handoff boundaries: a unit test of validator logic alone would miss losing the original row number before enqueue. Pass 2 mapping: Integration is the minimum level. Rejected higher levels: no behavior text requires real OS/network/subprocess; no AI/ML output dependency; no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run the pipeline against an in-process CSV input fixture with known missing required fields, using a real validator + transform orchestration + DLQ adapter wired to a local test sink; assert each rejected record includes the exact original CSV row number and payload.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: pure transformation logic (date parsing/normalization) for one function/object boundary. The behavior does not require module interaction to expose the core failure. Pass 2 mapping: Unit is minimum. Rejected higher levels: no concrete text requiring DB/S3/process/OS behavior, and no AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Table-driven unit test for the date-normalization function with ISO 8601, US, and European inputs (including edge cases like ambiguous day/month), asserting exact canonical output or explicit rejection semantics.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: real environment behavior (runtime memory profile, timeout behavior, streaming/backpressure under large input). In-process fakes can hide allocator pressure, GC behavior, and real timeout interactions. Pass 2 mapping: System is minimum. Rejected lower levels: unit/integration cannot faithfully expose OOM/timeout failure boundaries. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML output quality or multi-agent chain in behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the pipeline process end-to-end on a generated 500k-row CSV in a controlled environment with configured timeout and memory limits; assert successful completion before timeout and peak memory below threshold, failing on OOM, kill signals, or timeout breach.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: real HTTP/client behavior at infrastructure boundary (S3 503 surface, retryability classification, and timing/backoff behavior). Mocking the S3 client can always pass while missing SDK-level error mapping and retry semantics. Pass 2 mapping: System is minimum. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML dependency and no multi-agent sequence described.
behaviors.test_description: Run the pipeline against an HTTP-backed S3-compatible endpoint/test server that returns 503 for initial attempts then success; verify actual retry count and exponential backoff intervals from observed timestamps/logs, and confirm batch completion without terminal failure.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: process-lifecycle + persistence boundary (restart mid-batch with durable DB state and resume/idempotency semantics). This failure appears when real restart behavior interacts with transaction/offset/checkpoint handling. Pass 2 mapping: System is minimum. Rejected lower levels: integration without real restart can miss crash/restart ordering and commit boundary issues. Rejected higher levels: behavior has no AI/ML dependency or multi-agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Start a real pipeline run against test PostgreSQL, interrupt/terminate the process mid-batch after some commits, restart with same input/checkpoint state, then assert final table has exactly one copy per logical row (via unique business key/count checks) and no duplicate inserts.
